failure_modes.text: Confidence of ignorance makes less-informed decision-makers more confident than better-informed ones, because fewer contradictions and gaps allow a more coherent story to form.
failure_modes.mitigation: Ask what's missing before judging confidence: require the team to list information they do not have and would need to be more certain, before trusting the coherence of the current story.

failure_modes.text: The invisible denominator produces conclusions from a non-random sample without any awareness that the sample is incomplete — the missing cases simply do not appear.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the sample for completeness: before drawing conclusions, check whether the evidence base includes the cases that would naturally be invisible — failed attempts, silent customers, unreported errors.

failure_modes.text: Coherence as quality signal treats the internal consistency and narrative flow of an argument as evidence of its correctness — well-constructed wrong analyses feel more true than messy correct ones.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate coherence from correctness: when an argument feels convincing because it is well-structured, test it against messy contradictory evidence to see if the logic survives contact with reality.

premortem_questions.text: What evidence would we need to see to be confident in this conclusion — and do we know why that evidence is not in front of us?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Require an explicit 'what is missing?' audit before every significant conclusion

premortem_questions.text: Is this conclusion well-supported, or is it well-constructed? What is the difference in this case?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Separate the evaluation of logical coherence from the evaluation of empirical completeness

premortem_questions.text: If the person preparing this briefing had wanted to show us a different conclusion, what would they have included that is not here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: In advisory or briefing contexts, explicitly ask what is not in this presentation

heuristics.text: Before committing to any confident conclusion, explicitly audit what information is missing and whether the absence is random or structured by selection or incentive.
heuristics.source_quote: Audit the Denominator: explicitly ask 'what is not in this picture?'

heuristics.text: Treat high confidence from a coherent story as a scrutiny trigger, not a quality signal — WYSIATI predicts that the situations where confidence feels most warranted are the ones most likely to be wrong.
heuristics.source_quote: Pre-Mortem Before Confidence: when a conclusion feels certain, treat the certainty as a trigger for scrutiny

heuristics.text: Apply outside-view reference-class data to import evidence not in the current picture — base rates provide exactly the missing context that System 1's narrative would otherwise exclude.

curation_notes.summary: Wave 2 for WYSIATI focuses on the three most decision-relevant failure modes — confidence of ignorance, invisible denominator, and coherence-as-quality-signal — with audit-focused heuristics for each.
